---
title: "Robust model pieces"
subtitle: "Downweight outliers before they reach the GLM seam."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

A single bad time point — a swallow, a big head movement — can pull an
ordinary least-squares estimate toward itself, because OLS minimizes
*squared* residuals and large residuals dominate that sum. Robust
fitting limits this: estimate a residual scale that is itself resistant
to outliers, then downweight any time point whose residual is implausibly
large for that scale, and refit. The full estimator is `fmri_rlm(...)`;
this page opens up the two primitives it is built from so you can see
exactly what "downweight an outlier" means numerically.

## Robust Scale and Weights

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from fmrimod.robust import bisquare_weights, huber_weights, mad_scale

residuals = np.array([
    [0.10,  0.20],
    [0.00, -0.10],
    [0.20,  0.10],
    [5.00, -4.00],   # a visible outlier in both voxels
    [-0.10, 0.00],
])

scale = mad_scale(residuals, axis=0)
scale.round(3)
```

Huber weights shrink the outlier; bisquare weights can drop it completely.
Ordinary residuals retain weight near one.

```{python}
weights = pd.DataFrame({
    "residual_voxel_1": residuals[:, 0],
    "huber_voxel_1": huber_weights(residuals, scale)[:, 0],
    "bisquare_voxel_1": bisquare_weights(residuals, scale)[:, 0],
})
weights.round(3)
```

## Where This Fits

Use robust fitting when a small number of high-residual time points would
otherwise dominate a voxel-wise estimate. The authored model stays the same;
the estimator changes how much influence each time point gets during the
refit.

## Next

- [Linear model](linear-model.qmd)
- [Sketched and AR-aware models](sketched-ar-models.qmd)
